content.text: Code generation in an AI coding agent is the process by which an LLM produces new source code from a natural-language task description. Unlike a standalone autocomplete (which extends a single cursor position), agentic code generation is a multi-step pipeline: the agent assembles a context window from repository navigation results, constructs a carefully engineered prompt, submits it to the LLM, parses the generated output, verifies it, and optionally iterates when the output is syntactically invalid or fails tests.

The LLM is the generative core, but it is not the whole system. Token-efficient context selection determines what the model sees. Prompt engineering shapes how the model interprets the task. Output parsing extracts the code from the model's response. Verification gates catch errors before they reach the file system. Iterative refinement turns a first-pass failure into a correct result. Each of these components is as important as the model itself.

Code generation is where the agent's intelligence becomes tangible. All of the navigation, context assembly, and prompt engineering exist to feed this step correctly. A poorly assembled context or a weak prompt produces code that does not compile, does not match the existing codebase conventions, or silently does the wrong thing. The quality of code generation is the primary metric by which developers judge a coding agent.

The practical challenge is that LLMs are probabilistic: the same prompt run twice may produce different code. This non-determinism is managed, not eliminated. Setting temperature to 0 (or near 0) for code generation tasks minimizes variance without completely removing the model's ability to explore the solution space. Combining low temperature with a deterministic verification step (syntax check, type check, test run) produces a pipeline whose output is reliable even if the model's raw output is not.
An LLM generates a syntactically complete function in seconds; a developer writing from scratch takes minutes to hours for equivalent code.
When sibling files are included in the context, the model replicates existing naming conventions, error-handling patterns, and docstring styles without explicit instruction.
Repetitive scaffolding (CRUD operations, data class definitions, test stubs) is generated reliably without cognitive overhead.
A single LLM generates correct code in Python, TypeScript, Go, and Rust within the same session, without the developer needing to context-switch between language-specific tooling.
Given an implementation, the model generates unit tests that cover edge cases a developer might overlook, including boundary values, error paths, and type coercion cases.

LLM-generated code has a characteristic failure mode: it looks correct and compiles, but does something subtly wrong. The model may hallucinate an API that does not exist, use a deprecated parameter, or produce code that handles the happy path correctly but silently swallows errors in exception handlers. Static verification (type checking, linting) catches structural errors; only execution-based verification (running tests) catches semantic errors.

Context window limits impose hard constraints on generation quality. If the relevant implementation context exceeds the available token budget, the model generates code without seeing the interfaces it must conform to, producing type errors and import failures. The agent must therefore prioritize context: type signatures and function headers of direct dependencies are more valuable than full implementation bodies of distant modules.

The code generation pipeline has five stages. First, the agent retrieves relevant context from the codebase index (covered in Codebase Navigation). Second, it selects which context items to include in the prompt, respecting a token budget. The selection heuristic is: focal file first (full text), then direct-import type signatures, then semantically similar code as excerpts, then the task description, then output-space reserve.

Third, the agent assembles the prompt. The system prompt establishes the role ("You are an expert Python developer..."), the output format ("Respond with only the code, enclosed in a Python fenced code block"), and any project-specific constraints ("Use the existing
"). The user turn contains the retrieved context followed by the task description.

Fourth, the LLM generates a response. The agent sets temperature to 0 for determinism and uses stop sequences to prevent the model from generating explanatory text after the code block. Fifth, the agent parses the response: it extracts the code from between the fenced code block markers, validates the syntax, optionally runs type checking or linting, and optionally runs the test suite. If any verification step fails, the agent re-prompts with the error message appended, asking the model to fix the specific problem.

attrs.alt: LLM Code Generation Pipeline: Task Description flows through Context Selection, Prompt Assembly, LLM Inference, and Output Parsing & Validation, with a refinement loop back to Context Selection on failure.

content.text: The pipeline is linear from task to verified code, with a feedback loop on failure. The refinement loop is bounded: a maximum retry count (typically 3–5 attempts) prevents infinite loops when the model cannot fix a problem it repeatedly introduces. After the retry limit, the agent reports the failure to the user rather than silently producing broken code.

LLM code generation is well-suited for tasks with clear, local scope: implement a new function, add a method to an existing class, write unit tests for an existing implementation, generate a data model from a schema. It is less suited for tasks requiring deep architectural decisions (where the agent lacks the business context to make the right trade-off) or tasks requiring coordination across many files without a clear dependency structure.

For large-scope changes ("refactor the authentication system"), the agent should decompose the task into a sequence of small, independently verifiable generation subtasks rather than attempting to generate all changes in a single prompt. Each subtask generates code for one function or class, verifies it in isolation, and then the agent proceeds to the next.
The following shows a complete code generation pipeline with context assembly, LLM call, output parsing, and iterative refinement.

# Minimal code generation pipeline
# pip install anthropic

import ast
import re
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def extract_code(response_text: str) -> str | None:
    """
    Extract the first fenced Python code block from the model's response.
    Returns None if no block is found.
    """
    pattern = r"```(?:python)?\n(.*?)\n```"
    match = re.search(pattern, response_text, re.DOTALL)
    return match.group(1).strip() if match else None

def syntax_check(code: str) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """Return (is_valid, error_message)."""
    try:
        ast.parse(code)
        return True, ""
    except SyntaxError as e:
        return False, f"SyntaxError at line {e.lineno}: {e.msg}"

def generate_code(
    task: str,
    context: str,
    max_retries: int = 3,
) -> str:
    """
    Generate and verify code for the given task.
    Retries up to max_retries times if syntax validation fails.
    """
    messages = [
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"## Repository context\n\n{context}\n\n"
                f"## Task\n\n{task}\n\n"
                "Respond with ONLY the implementation, enclosed in a Python "
                "fenced code block (```python ... ```). No prose before or after."
            ),
        }
    ]
    system = (
        "You are an expert Python developer. "
        "Write production-quality code that matches the style and conventions "
        "of the existing codebase. Use type annotations. "
        "Do not include import statements already present in the context."
    )

    for attempt in range(1, max_retries + 1):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=2048,
            temperature=0,   # deterministic for code
            system=system,
            messages=messages,
        )
        raw = response.content[0].text
        code = extract_code(raw)

        if code is None:
            # No code block found — ask the model to wrap its output
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})
            messages.append({
                "role": "user",
                "content": "Your response did not contain a fenced code block. "
                           "Please rewrite it with the code inside ```python ... ```.",
            })
            continue

        ok, error = syntax_check(code)
        if ok:
            return code

        # Syntax error: append error and re-prompt
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})
        messages.append({
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"The code has a syntax error:\n\n{error}\n\nPlease fix it.",
        })

    raise RuntimeError(
        f"Code generation failed after {max_retries} attempts. "
        "Last error: " + error
    )

Context selection is the most impactful variable in generation quality. The following shows a token-aware context assembler that prioritizes focal-file content and type signatures over implementation bodies.

# Token-efficient context selection for code generation
# Approximation: 1 token ≈ 4 characters for English/code text

from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass
class ContextItem:
    content: str
    priority: int   # lower = higher priority
    label: str      # used as a comment header in the final context

def build_context(
    focal_file: str,
    import_signatures: list[tuple[str, str]],   # [(module_path, signatures_text)]
    semantic_excerpts: list[tuple[str, str]],    # [(file_path, excerpt_text)]
    token_budget: int = 3500,
) -> str:
    """
    Assemble a context string within a token budget.
    Order: focal file (full) > import signatures > semantic excerpts.
    """
    CHARS_PER_TOKEN = 4
    char_budget = token_budget * CHARS_PER_TOKEN

    items: list[ContextItem] = []

    # Priority 0: focal file — always include, highest priority
    focal_text = Path(focal_file).read_text(errors="replace")
    items.append(ContextItem(
        content=focal_text,
        priority=0,
        label=f"# FILE: {focal_file}",
    ))

    # Priority 1: type signatures of direct imports
    for mod_path, sig_text in import_signatures:
        items.append(ContextItem(
            content=sig_text,
            priority=1,
            label=f"# SIGNATURES: {mod_path}",
        ))

    # Priority 2: semantically related code excerpts
    for file_path, excerpt in semantic_excerpts:
        items.append(ContextItem(
            content=excerpt,
            priority=2,
            label=f"# RELATED: {file_path}",
        ))

    items.sort(key=lambda x: x.priority)

    parts = []
    used_chars = 0
    for item in items:
        chunk = f"{item.label}\n{item.content}\n"
        if used_chars + len(chunk) > char_budget:
            # Include a truncated version if partial content is still useful
            remaining = char_budget - used_chars
            if remaining > 200:
                truncated = chunk[:remaining] + "\n# ... (truncated)\n"
                parts.append(truncated)
            break
        parts.append(chunk)
        used_chars += len(chunk)

    return "\n".join(parts)
Prompt engineering for code generation requires precision about the output format. The following shows a few-shot example structure that consistently produces parseable output.

# Few-shot prompt template for code generation
# Few-shot examples teach the model the expected output format

FEW_SHOT_SYSTEM = """
You are an expert Python developer. Given a task description and repository 
context, implement the requested function. Follow these rules exactly:

1. Output ONLY the function implementation inside a Python code block.
2. Do not repeat import statements that already appear in the context.
3. Use the same logging pattern visible in the context (never print()).
4. Include a one-line docstring summarizing what the function does.
5. Use type annotations for all parameters and the return type.

Example of correct output format:
```python
def calculate_discount(price: float, rate: float) -> float:
    """Return the discounted price after applying the given percentage rate."""
    if not 0.0 <= rate <= 1.0:
        raise ValueError(f"Discount rate must be between 0 and 1, got {rate}")
    return price * (1.0 - rate)
```

Example of incorrect output (do not do this):
Here is the implementation you requested:
```python
...
```
This function calculates the discount by...
"""

def make_generation_prompt(task: str, context: str) -> str:
    return (
        f"## Context\n\n{context}\n\n"
        f"## Task\n\n{task}\n\n"
        "Implement the function now:"
    )
A production coding agent for a Python microservice is asked to add a new endpoint. The following shows the full pipeline: context assembly, generation, syntax check, and test-run verification.

# Production pipeline: generate a FastAPI endpoint with test verification
import subprocess
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path

def generate_and_verify_endpoint(
    task: str,
    focal_file: str,
    test_command: list[str],
    max_retries: int = 4,
) -> str:
    """
    Full pipeline: context assembly → generation → syntax check → test run.
    Returns the verified generated code.
    """
    # 1. Build context from the focal file + related modules
    context = build_context(
        focal_file=focal_file,
        import_signatures=extract_import_signatures(focal_file),
        semantic_excerpts=semantic_search(task, n=4),
    )

    error_feedback = ""
    for attempt in range(1, max_retries + 1):
        task_with_feedback = task
        if error_feedback:
            task_with_feedback += f"\n\nPrevious attempt failed:\n{error_feedback}\nPlease fix the issue."

        # 2. Generate
        code = generate_code(
            task=task_with_feedback,
            context=context,
            max_retries=1,  # inner retries only for parse errors
        )

        # 3. Syntax check (already done inside generate_code, but kept explicit)
        ok, err = syntax_check(code)
        if not ok:
            error_feedback = err
            continue

        # 4. Write to a temp file and run tests
        target = Path(focal_file)
        backup = target.read_text()
        try:
            # Append the generated code to the target file
            with open(focal_file, "a") as f:
                f.write("\n\n" + code)

            result = subprocess.run(
                test_command,
                capture_output=True,
                text=True,
                timeout=60,
            )
            if result.returncode == 0:
                print(f"Tests passed on attempt {attempt}")
                return code

            # Tests failed: restore file, collect error for next attempt
            target.write_text(backup)
            error_feedback = (
                result.stdout[-2000:]  # last 2 000 chars of test output
                + result.stderr[-1000:]
            )
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            target.write_text(backup)
            error_feedback = "Test suite timed out (60 s)"
        except Exception as e:
            target.write_text(backup)
            error_feedback = str(e)

    raise RuntimeError(
        f"Failed to generate correct code after {max_retries} attempts.\n"
        f"Last error:\n{error_feedback}"
    )

The most common mistake is treating LLM output as trusted code. Generated code must always pass a verification gate before being written to disk. An agent that appends generated code to a file without a syntax check can corrupt the file, making subsequent generation attempts harder because the AST parser will fail on the malformed file.

# Wrong: write LLM output directly to disk without verification
def bad_generate(task, context, filepath):
    code = llm_call(task, context)   # raw text from model
    with open(filepath, 'a') as f:
        f.write(code)   # may be invalid Python

# Right: verify before writing; restore backup on failure
def good_generate(task, context, filepath):
    backup = Path(filepath).read_text()
    code = extract_code(llm_call(task, context))
    if code is None:
        raise ValueError("No code block in response")
    ok, error = syntax_check(code)
    if not ok:
        raise ValueError(f"Invalid syntax: {error}")
    # Only write after verification passes
    with open(filepath, 'a') as f:
        f.write('\n\n' + code)
    # Optionally run tests; restore backup if they fail
    if not run_tests():
        Path(filepath).write_text(backup)
        raise RuntimeError("Tests failed after applying generated code")

A second common mistake is an unbounded retry loop. Without a maximum retry count the agent can burn through API quota in a tight loop when the model repeatedly generates the same buggy output. Always bound retries and surface the failure to the user.
Set temperature to 0 for code generation.
Non-zero temperature introduces unnecessary variance. If the model needs to explore (e.g., for test generation where diversity is desirable), use temperature 0.2–0.4, not higher.
Specify the exact output format in the system prompt.
Demand a fenced code block with no prose before or after. Prose in the response forces the output parser to do more work and increases the chance of a parse failure.
Include a representative sibling file in context.
A file from the same module that follows the project's conventions is the single most effective way to get the model to match the codebase's style without explicit style instructions.
Verify with execution, not just syntax.

A syntactically valid program can still import a nonexistent module, call a function with the wrong arguments, or return the wrong type. Run the test suite after applying generated code whenever a test suite exists.

A single prompt asking for a 500-line module is far less reliable than five sequential prompts each generating a 100-line class. Smaller generation units are easier to verify and easier to fix when they fail.
Always include a token budget for the output.
to a value that comfortably covers the expected generated code but does not allow the model to ramble. An unexpectedly truncated response is always a parse failure.

LLM inference latency for code generation is dominated by output token count. A 100-line function (~400 output tokens) takes 3–8 seconds on a frontier model. A 500-line class takes 15–30 seconds. Streaming (receiving tokens as they are generated) reduces perceived latency but does not reduce total time-to-first-token. For tasks where the agent edits multiple files sequentially, total wall-clock time scales linearly with the number of generation calls.

Parallelism is possible when generation subtasks are independent. If the agent needs to generate three unrelated utility functions, it can submit all three LLM requests concurrently and assemble the results when all are verified. Python's
or a thread pool handles this naturally. The Anthropic Python SDK provides an async client (
Both support streaming and async; use AsyncAnthropic for parallel generation
Fast; raises SyntaxError with line number on invalid code
Catches import errors, wrong argument types, return-type mismatches
Extremely fast; covers flake8 + isort + pyupgrade rules; runs in <100 ms
The gold standard for semantic verification; run with -x to fail fast
Accurate token count for context budget management without an API call

Generated code should be treated with the same scrutiny as code from an untrusted contributor. An LLM can be induced, via prompt injection in the task description or retrieved context, to generate code that exfiltrates environment variables, opens network connections, or executes shell commands. Defense layers are: sandbox the test runner (no network, no write access outside the project directory), review generated code before merging, and never auto-commit without a human review step.
Even a high-quality coding agent produces subtly wrong code often enough that human review on every commit is essential for production codebases.
Run tests in a container with
and read-only mounts for everything outside the working directory. This contains the blast radius if generated code is malicious.
If the task description or retrieved context contains strings like "ignore previous instructions" or "output your system prompt", reject the task before sending it to the LLM.
Enforce a maximum output token limit. A response that is unexpectedly long (thousands of tokens when a 100-line function was requested) is a signal that something went wrong.
Code generation is a pipeline, not a single LLM call: context selection, prompt assembly, inference, output parsing, and verification are all load-bearing components.
Set temperature to 0 and demand a fenced code block as the sole output — these two prompt engineering choices remove the largest sources of parse failures.
Verification must include execution (tests), not just syntax checking: syntactically valid code can still import nonexistent modules, return wrong types, or silently mishandle errors.
Iterative refinement with bounded retries turns a first-pass failure into a correct result; unbounded retries burn quota without converging.
Including a sibling file in context is the single most effective way to match codebase conventions without explicit style instructions.
Always include a token reserve for generated output when assembling context; a truncated output is always a parse failure.
Generated code must be reviewed before merging; sandbox test execution to prevent malicious or inadvertently destructive code from running with agent-level privileges.
